title: Fitting architrave to a door opening
subtitle: Mitre-jointed softwood architrave around a new or re-lined internal door
excerpt: How to fit architrave around an internal door opening, covering marking a consistent reveal, cutting 45-degree mitres at the head corners, pinning and gluing the joints, and filling for a smooth painted finish.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. The Practical Carpenter and Joiner (1891).

glossaryTerms.definition: The narrow strip of door lining left visible between the lining edge and the face of the architrave. A consistent reveal of 6 to 8 mm on all three sides of the opening (both legs and the head) is the first mark to set before cutting any timber. A pencil line scribed at this distance using the combination square gives the inner edge of the architrave on all three sides.

The 45-degree angled cut made at the top of each architrave leg and at each end of the head piece, so the two pieces meet in a right angle at the corner. A good mitre on architrave closes face to face with no gap. The joint is glued and pinned through both faces to prevent it opening as the timber moves seasonally.

content.text: Architrave covers the joint between the door lining and the surrounding plaster. The key to a clean result is a consistent
on all three sides before any timber is cut, and a tight
at each corner where the legs meet the head. Both can be achieved with hand tools in a morning.

Architrave moulding to match the existing doors in the house, long enough for both legs and the head with waste for the mitres. Standard torus or ogee sections are stocked by most timber merchants in softwood; MDF pre-primed versions are also available. Forty-millimetre oval wire nails or panel pins for pinning to the lining and the wall. PVA wood glue for the mitre joints. Fine surface filler or decorator's caulk for the outer edge before painting.
Step 1, mark the reveal on all three sides

Set the combination square to 6 mm and run a pencil line along all three inner edges of the door lining: both vertical legs and the horizontal head. This line is the inner edge of the architrave on all three sides. Check that the lining is flush with the plaster surface before marking. If the lining stands proud, plane it flat. If it sits back, the plaster behind the architrave will need to be made good before painting.
Step 2, cut and fit the two leg pieces

Cut the bottom of each leg square: the legs sit on the floor or on a plinth block. Mark the mitre cut height on each leg by holding the piece in position with its inner edge on the reveal line and marking where the reveal line on the head lining crosses the face. Cut the mitre at 45 degrees at that mark, with the short point of the mitre on the reveal side. Pin each leg loosely to the lining with two or three nails, not fully driven, so the leg can be shifted if the head mitre needs adjusting.
Step 3, cut and fit the head piece

Cut one mitre on the head piece, hold it against the leg mitre, and mark the second mitre from the opposite leg. Cut the second mitre. The head should sit so that both mitres close tight at the same time. If one mitre is open at the face, trim the long point with the block plane one or two shavings at a time. When both mitres close cleanly, apply PVA to both faces of each mitre joint and pin through the mitre from both directions: one nail through the leg face into the head, one through the head face into the leg.
Step 4, pin out and make good

Drive all the nails home with the pin hammer and punch the heads just below the surface. Fill the nail holes and the outer edge of the architrave where it meets the plaster with fine surface filler or caulk. Allow to dry, sand lightly, and prime any bare timber before painting. Mitre joints may open slightly if the timber dries after painting: run a fine bead of caulk into an open joint rather than re-cutting it.

On a wide moulding section (over 70 mm), fix the outer edge of the architrave to the wall as well as the lining: it spans beyond the lining and needs its own fixing. On a lath-and-plaster wall, locate the studs before nailing. If the door has a plinth block at the base of each leg, the legs sit on top of the block and are cut square at both ends: there are no mitres on the legs, only at each end of the head.

Architrave in a heated house shrinks slightly each winter as indoor humidity drops. Glued mitre joints resist this better than pinned-only joints. Repaint the architrave every four to six years: bare timber at the mitre joint absorbs moisture and opens the joint faster. Do not fill an open mitre with caulk on top of old caulk without removing the old fill first. Built-up caulk eventually cracks out.

attrs.symptom: Mitre joint is open at the face when the head is in place
attrs.cause: The head piece is very slightly long, pushing the leg mitres out of square, or the mitre angle was not quite 45 degrees.
attrs.fix: Remove the head and plane the long point of the open mitre with the block plane, one or two shavings at a time. Test the fit after each pass. Plane only the cut mitre surface, not the face of the moulding.

attrs.symptom: Outer edge of the architrave stands away from the plaster
attrs.cause: The wall surface is not flat, or the door lining sits further from the wall than the plaster face.
attrs.fix: Fill the gap between the architrave and the plaster with decorator's caulk rather than surface filler: caulk stays flexible and does not crack as the wall moves. Apply after the architrave is fully pinned and before painting.
